summary: DHH announces that ONCE #1 (Campfire) ships with zero JavaScript or CSS build steps, serving 68 individual JS files and multiple CSS files directly to browsers via import maps and HTTP/2. He argues that modern browsers now natively support everything developers previously needed transpilers and bundlers for — including CSS nesting and variables — while still achieving a perfect PageSpeed Insights score of 100. He acknowledges bundlers still have their place for large SPAs but celebrates that they're no longer necessary. The post frames this as the culmination of a long journey through webpack and esbuild, with simplicity as the ultimate destination.
key_points: ONCE #1 serves 68 unbundled JavaScript files via import maps with HTTP/2, paying minimal penalty for individual file loading while gaining fine-grained cache expiration
Modern vanilla CSS now supports nesting and variables natively across Chrome, Safari, and Firefox, eliminating the need for preprocessors
The application achieves a perfect 100 PageSpeed Insights score despite serving many small individual files without bundling
View Source once again shows the exact same code developers write in their editors, restoring a fundamental web transparency that was lost
Bundlers and TypeScript remain valid choices for large SPAs — the point is they're no longer mandatory
money_quotes: The dream has come true. It's now possible to build fast, modern web applications without transpiling or bundling either JavaScript or CSS.
Looking at any of those individual files via View Source reveals exactly the same source code that I see in my editor. It's been a long time since JavaScript developers and designers could say that.
To people who haven't seen modern, vanilla CSS, something like this might look like it's from the future. Nesting?! Variables?! Yes. Yes. It's good!
When some JavaScript people see arguments like this, it seems like their brain just turns into a red mush of rage. Like I've insulted their religion and all its holy prophets.
All I'm demonstrating here is that it's no longer necessary to live and work like this. You can choose something different.
I'm a firm believer that complexity ought to be a temporary price we pay for progress. The final destination, for me, has always been simplicity.
Nothing is simpler than sending a plain-text JavaScript or CSS file straight to a browser and watch the magic play.
key_insight: Modern browsers have finally caught up to the point where transpiling and bundling are optional, not mandatory — and shipping plain JavaScript and CSS directly is both simpler and performant enough for production applications.
